title: Pâte de fruit,raspberry jellies
subtitle: Set fruit jellies cut into squares and rolled in sugar
excerpt: French set-fruit confectionery,a thick raspberry purée cooked with sugar to the soft-ball stage, set with apple pectin, poured into a frame and cut into glittering sugar-rolled squares. Bright, tart, and unmistakable. Requires a sugar thermometer and patience: the boil is short but the set is overnight.
sourceNotes: Pâte de fruit is documented in nineteenth-century French confectionery manuals,Pierre Lacam, Le Mémorial Historique et Géographique de la Pâtisserie (1890; out of copyright) is the canonical reference and gives the foundational fruit-to-sugar ratios. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 covers the related British fruit-paste preserves. USDA / NCHFP guidance on pectin handling for set preserves. The 115°C target is the modern confectionery refinement; older sources rely on a cold-water plate test, which still works as a backup if the thermometer fails.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The jellies need an overnight set. Make the day before serving and roll in sugar 1 hour before plating.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: soft-ball stage, 115°C,the target sugar temperature on the thermometer. A digital probe is more accurate than a clip-on glass thermometer for the short window.

recipeTools.notes: Heavy-bottomed; pâte de fruit catches on a thin pan.
A digital probe gives quicker readings than a clip-on glass thermometer.
20 cm × 20 cm tin lined with cling film, or a confectionery frame.

glossaryTerms.definition: 115°C on the sugar thermometer,the cooking stage at which a teaspoon of syrup dropped into cold water forms a soft, pliable ball you can squash between finger and thumb. The target for pâte de fruit, soft fudge, fondant, and Italian meringue. Above 120°C the syrup moves to firm-ball; the difference is only five degrees but pâte de fruit cooked past 118°C sets too hard to cut cleanly.

content.text: Pâte de fruit is the French set-fruit confectionery: a thick raspberry purée cooked to the soft-ball stage with sugar and pectin, poured into a frame, set overnight, cut into squares, and rolled in caster sugar. Bright tart-sweet, glassy at the surface, dense and chewy inside. The work is short; the set is long. Plan the cook for the afternoon and cut the squares the next morning.

Safety reading first. Sugar above 130°C causes severe burns. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed. Keep children and pets out of the kitchen while the pan is on the heat. Take the time to lay out everything before you start the boil,the cook is fast and there is no time to fetch a missing tool.

Two technical points. The pectin must be whisked into a portion of the sugar before it goes in the pan,added straight, it clumps and refuses to dissolve. And the cook to 115°C is the difference between cuttable jelly and grainy paste; trust the thermometer, not the colour. Domestic hobs run hot; an instant-read digital probe is more accurate than a clip-on glass thermometer in the short window.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: yellow pectin if available, otherwise standard apple pectin

content.text: Method
Prepare the purée and frame
Line the 20 cm square tin with cling film, pressing it into the corners so the surface is flat. Cling film is the standard release; baking paper sticks. Set aside.

Push the raspberries through a fine-mesh sieve into the saucepan, working the back of a ladle in circles to extract all the juice and pulp without the seeds. You should have about 400 g of seed-free purée.
Measure 50 g of the granulated sugar into a small bowl and whisk in the pectin. This is the only way the pectin disperses without clumping.
Cook to 115°C

Set the pan over medium heat and warm the purée to a gentle simmer, stirring with a silicone spatula. Sprinkle the pectin-sugar mixture in evenly, whisking constantly until smooth. Now add the remaining 450 g granulated sugar in three parts, whisking between each. Bring the mixture to a steady boil.

Clip on the sugar thermometer (or insert the digital probe),tip in the syrup, not touching the pan bottom. Cook at a steady boil, stirring constantly with the spatula to prevent catching, until the temperature reaches the
soft-ball stage at 115°C
. The mixture darkens slightly, thickens, and pulls cleanly from the bottom of the pan in a strip when the spatula passes through. Trust the thermometer,the colour cue is secondary.
Take the pan off the heat the second the thermometer reads 115°C. Whisk in the lemon juice,the acid sharpens the flavour and helps the pectin set.
Pour and set

Pour the hot jelly into the lined tin, tapping the tin gently on the bench to level the surface and pop any bubbles. Leave to cool at room temperature for 30 minutes,do not cover or refrigerate, which traps moisture on the surface. Set overnight at room temperature, around 12 hours, until firm enough to cut cleanly.
Cut and sugar

Tip the set jelly out of the tin onto a board dusted with caster sugar. Peel the cling film away. Cut into 2.5 cm squares with a long sharp knife, dipping the blade in hot water and drying between cuts for clean edges. Toss the squares in the remaining caster sugar to coat all sides, including the cut faces. The sugar layer keeps them from sticking and gives the glittering finish.
Store between layers of baking paper in an airtight tin. They keep for 2 weeks at room temperature. The sugar coating dissolves slowly into the surface over time; re-roll if needed before serving.
